description: Base-rate dataset of regulatory actions against prediction markets, sports-betting exchanges, and CFTC-adjacent DCMs. Curated for relevance to Polymarket's risk profile. Category-error cases (FTX fraud, Binance AML) excluded deliberately - those shape a different base rate.

relevance_tags.high: Same business model (event-contract / prediction market / sports DCM) operating under same/similar US regulatory regime
relevance_tags.medium: Similar retail derivatives / crypto exchange under US regulatory scrutiny
relevance_tags.low: Tangential - included for context, not base-rate counting

outcomes.shutdown_us: Forced to cease US operations; users geofenced or platform sunset
outcomes.fine_and_continue: Monetary penalty, operations continue substantially unchanged
outcomes.geofence_and_continue: US partially blocked; product continues elsewhere or with restrictions
outcomes.acquired: Sold to regulated entity before resolution
outcomes.restructured: Forced to obtain license / change corporate structure; operations continue under new regime

events.notes: Closest direct analogue. Non-US-regulated event contracts marketed to US retail; CFTC alleged illegal off-exchange binary options. US users cut off; company wound down 2013 due to related financial issues.

CFTC rescinded 2014 no-action letter; Victoria University of Wellington / Aristotle forced to wind down. Ongoing federal litigation reinstated operations while case proceeds. Direct signal on CFTC willingness to close prediction markets even with prior permissive guidance.

CFTC moved to prohibit congressional election contracts; Kalshi prevailed at DC Circuit (Sept 2024) allowing listing. Establishes case law that event contracts can't be categorically blocked. Key precedent for Polymarket if it pursues CFTC-regulated venue.
events.source: KalshiEX LLC v. CFTC, D.D.C. Case 1:23-cv-03257

events.notes: $1.4M settlement. Required to geoblock US users from non-registered contracts but permitted to continue non-US operations. Established US-geofenced model Polymarket still uses.

12+ states (NJ, NV, IL, MI, MD, OH, TX, CO, AZ others) sent cease-and-desist regarding sports markets. Polymarket geofenced affected states individually; US non-sports remained available via QCEX path. Ongoing.

Arizona filed criminal charges against Polymarket operators under state unlawful-gambling statutes. First criminal case against a prediction market in the modern era. Key escalation indicator. Outcome pending.
Approved as a CFTC DCM. Shows the regulated path works for sports/event products - now the model Polymarket aims for via QCEX.
CFTC's attempt to block election contracts defeated on appeal. Pending Supreme Court cert consideration. Major precedent limiting CFTC's categorical-ban authority.
events.source: KalshiEX LLC v. CFTC, D.C. Cir. 2024

events.notes: DFS model survived via licensing; illustrates state-by-state licensing pathway for sports-contingent products. Not a shutdown outcome but shows state regulator aggressiveness.
DFS operators required to obtain Nevada gaming license; DK/FanDuel geofenced NV. Precedent for operator continuing in 49 states while one state forces shutdown.

Offshore sportsbooks served as the counterfactual market. Operated under pressure; payment rails occasionally disrupted; substantially continued US service. Shows the floor even under adversarial enforcement.
P2P sports exchange pursuing state-by-state sports-wagering licenses. Slower scale, compliant model. Contrasts with Polymarket's CFTC-route ambition.

Not a regulatory event; included for token-reflexivity reference. Ticker OPINION saw ~95% drawdown post-TGE; operator entity effectively discontinued. Cited as single-datapoint basis for token-failure risk.
Preemptive geoblock before formal action; structural template Polymarket follows for non-US operations while pursuing regulated US subsidiary.

CFTC-registered DCM survived FTX parent collapse via bankruptcy sale to MIAX. Precedent that DCM registration has standalone value even under adverse parent events. Relevant for QCEX asset protection.
Operating CFTC-registered DCM offering binary event contracts for over a decade. Baseline proof point that the DCM route for event contracts functions stably.
UK jurisdictional analogue - licensed under gambling regime rather than securities. Illustrates alternative regulatory model. Included for international context.

CFTC granted academic no-action relief to University of Iowa for real-money political prediction markets in 1992/1993 (refined 1993). Continuously operating under that letter for 30+ years without enforcement. Strongest single base-rate anchor for 'CFTC tolerates prediction markets under constrained structure'.
events.source: CFTC No-Action Letter to University of Iowa (1993)

events.notes: First CFTC-registered DCM for binary event contracts (2004). Acquired by IG Group in 2007 and rebranded Nadex. Shows DCM-registration pathway produces durable, acquirable regulated-venue asset - the template Polymarket's QCEX deal relies on.
events.source: CFTC DCM order 2004; IG Group press release 2007

events.notes: Forecast Foundation deployed geoblocked front-end to avoid CFTC exposure after CFTC 2019 guidance implied binary event contracts on unregistered venues were illegal. Protocol continues; mainstream UX effectively dead. Informs the 'geofence as floor' outcome probability.
events.source: Forecast Foundation statements 2020; CFTC 2019 interpretive guidance

events.notes: Nov 2024 FBI search of Shayne Coplan's apartment related to 2022 CFTC settlement compliance; DOJ declined to bring charges in 2025. Highest-profile federal criminal investigation of Polymarket concluded without action. Relevant favourable signal; note Polymarket-on-Polymarket tautology caveat.

events.entity: DraftKings / FanDuel - NY AG DFS C&D (2015)
events.notes: NY AG Schneiderman issued C&Ds declaring DFS illegal gambling; operators initially geoblocked NY, then legislature passed DFS-licensing statute (2016) and operators resumed under license. Paradigmatic 'state forces licensing, product continues' path.
events.source: People v. FanDuel Inc., NY Sup. Ct. 2015; NY S8153 (2016)

events.notes: TX AG Paxton issued opinion that paid DFS constitutes illegal gambling; DK/FD withdrew from Texas. State remained closed as of 2026. Exemplar of 'state closes, operator continues in other states' outcome - directly relevant to Polymarket's state-by-state geofence pattern.
IL AG Madigan opined DFS is illegal gambling (Dec 2015); operators paused, then Illinois Fantasy Contests Act (2019) legalised under licensing. Same restructuring pathway as NY.
MA AG Healey issued regulations requiring age limits, disclosure, marketing restrictions rather than outright ban. DK/FD complied and continued. Shows lower-friction state restructuring outcome.
Murphy v. NCAA (2018) struck PASPA; NJ launched licensed sports betting within weeks. Template for the state-by-state sports legalisation wave. Favourable-restructuring outcome at scale.
NY launched mobile sports betting under 51% tax rate. High-friction licensing but operationally open. Relevant as 'regulated but punitive economics' outcome - a Base-world variant.

events.entity: California Prop 27 (online sports betting ballot) failure (2022)
events.notes: Prop 27 to legalise online sports betting defeated 82-18. California mobile sports betting remains closed through 2026. Largest single-state 'closed' outcome for the category - adverse-pathway analogue.

2021 Seminole compact (giving tribe online sports betting monopoly) was enjoined then upheld on appeal (West Flagler v. Haaland, D.C. Cir. 2023). Cert denied 2023. Illustrates multi-year litigation to favourable restructuring outcome.
events.source: West Flagler Assocs. v. Haaland, 71 F.4th 1059 (D.C. Cir. 2023)

events.notes: $30M settlement; Kraken discontinued US staking product but continued trading operations. Adjacent-regime base-rate input on 'fine + product-scope curtailment, core business continues'.

$100M CFTC/FinCEN civil penalty; founders pleaded to BSA violations (probation, no prison for core founders). Exchange geofenced US, continued global operations. Closest crypto-derivatives analogue to Polymarket's CFTC posture.
events.source: CFTC Press Release 8412-21; SDNY U.S. v. Hayes et al.

events.notes: $100M settlement requiring registration of lending product; BlockFi subsequently failed (2022) for FTX-exposure reasons, not the SEC matter. Tagged low-relevance because the lending-product regime differs substantially from event contracts; retained for base-rate context.

SEC sued Coinbase June 2023 alleging unregistered exchange operation; SEC voluntarily dismissed Feb 2025 under new administration. Classified no_action on final-outcome basis. Adjacent-regime signal on enforcement reversibility.
events.source: SEC v. Coinbase, S.D.N.Y. Case 1:23-cv-04738 (dismissed Feb 2025)

events.notes: $505M settlement (forfeiture + penalty) for unlicensed money transmission; OKX continued global operations and announced US re-entry plans. Recent data point on crypto-exchange fine-and-continue pathway.

$42.5M combined CFTC settlement over stablecoin reserve misrepresentations and illegal retail off-exchange commodity transactions. Operations continued. Low-relevance - business model differs - included for completeness.

events.entity: Polymarket - EU MiFID / CySEC licensing path
events.notes: Polymarket pursuing EU licensing for regulated euro-denominated product. International context; not a US base-rate input.

CFTC approved Cantor Futures Exchange to list motion-picture box-office receipt contracts in April 2010. MPAA lobbied Congress; Dodd-Frank §721(c) (enacted July 2010) specifically banned 'motion picture box office receipts or any index, measure, value, or data related to such receipts' from DCM listing. Only clean precedent of a CFTC-approved event-contract product killed by targeted federal legislation. Cuts against the assumption that a favourable CFTC posture is durable against political intervention.
events.source: CFTC DCM order April 2010; Pub. L. 111-203 §721(c) (2010)

events.notes: Parallel CFTC approval to Cantor in April 2010 for box-office receipt contracts; killed by the same Dodd-Frank §721(c) clause three months later. Counted as a distinct data point because the DCM entity was separate, though the causal event is identical to Cantor.
events.source: CFTC DCM order April 2010; Pub. L. 111-203 §721(c) (2010)

events.entity: Kalshi - sports event contracts / state AG wave
events.regulator: Multiple state gaming regulators + federal courts
events.notes: Kalshi launched Super Bowl / NCAA / NFL event contracts in early 2025 under its CFTC DCM registration. NJ, NV, MD, MT, OH and others issued cease-and-desists asserting state gaming jurisdiction. Kalshi sued and won preliminary injunctions in NJ (D.N.J. 2025) and NV (D. Nev. 2025) on federal-preemption grounds. Direct parallel to Polymarket's state AG matter and the single most decision-relevant ongoing case; favourable interim signal but unresolved on the merits.
events.source: KalshiEX LLC v. Flaherty, D.N.J. 2025; KalshiEX LLC v. Hendrick, D. Nev. 2025

events.notes: CFTC-registered DCM for event contracts launched mid-2024 as Interactive Brokers subsidiary; operating without enforcement action. Additional evidence that the DCM-registration pathway for event contracts is open and being used by regulated incumbents, the same route Polymarket's QCEX deal targets.

Cantor rebranded its DCM as CX Futures Exchange after the 2010 box-office-receipt ban and has operated continuously as a CFTC-registered DCM since, at low volume. Evidence that DCM registration is durable even when a parent product is extinguished by federal action - the entity-level licence survives the product-level shutdown.

$4.3B DOJ/CFTC settlement (global Binance); Binance.US continued under US compliance monitor. Tagged low-relevance - AML-centric conduct rather than product-regime dispute. Retained for base-rate completeness.
